''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}

{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt) }}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|lt=Memphis}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|25th Schemu II|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|5th Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine|lt=Elephantine}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
({{ill|9th Peret II|de|Peret (Altes Ägypten)|lt=9th Peret II|lt_de=9. Peret II}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I

April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt) }}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|lt=Memphis}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|25th Schemu II|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|5th Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine|lt=Elephantine}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
({{ill|9th Peret II|de|Peret (Altes Ägypten)|lt=9th Peret II|lt_de=9. Peret II}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I

24th to 25th April |27th Schemu III
3rd to 4th July |{{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Astronomical data 28. Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * [[:de:Imi-chet-Sopdet|Imi-chet-Sopdet]] == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . == References == {{draft categories|[[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]][[category:new pages]] [[de:Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] }}[[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]]

The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}

April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * [[:de:Imi-chet-Sopdet|Imi-chet-Sopdet]] == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . == References == {{draft categories|[[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]][[category:new pages]] [[de:Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:user:immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] }}[[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}

Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * [[:de:Imi-chet-Sopdet|Imi-chet-Sopdet]] == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt) }}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|lt=Memphis}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|25th Schemu II|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|5th Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine|lt=Elephantine}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
({{ill|9th Peret II|de|Peret (Altes Ägypten)|lt=9th Peret II|lt_de=9. Peret II}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I

19th to 20th April |21st Peret II

23rd to 24th April |{{ill|9th Schemu III|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=9th Schemu III|lt_de=9. Schemu III}}

April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * [[:de:Imi-chet-Sopdet|Imi-chet-Sopdet]] == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt) }}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|lt=Memphis}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|25th Schemu II|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|5th Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine|lt=Elephantine}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June

Peret II}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. ''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt) }}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|lt=Memphis}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|25th Schemu II|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II}}) |'''23rd June'''

Peret II}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * [[:de:Imi-chet-Sopdet|Imi-chet-Sopdet]] == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt) }}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility

({{ill|25th Schemu II|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II}}) |'''23rd June'''

Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * [[:de:Imi-chet-Sopdet|Imi-chet-Sopdet]] == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . == References == {{draft categories|[[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:draft:Sopdet (constellation)]] [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Kk]] [[de:Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:user:immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] }}[[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt) }}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}

Peret II}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{Ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt) }}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|lt=Memphis}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|25th Schemu II|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|5th Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine|lt=Elephantine}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
({{ill|9th Peret II|de|Peret (Altes Ägypten)|lt=9th Peret II|lt_de=9. Peret II}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I
19th to 20th January |11th Achet IV
19th to 20th April |21st Peret II
28th to 29th June |{{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |1869 BC |6th Achet III
20th to 21st January
Altitude 35.5°Actual culmination 40.7° about 2.25 hours after sunset. |6th Peret II
20th to 21st April
Visibility arc 8.6° |16th Peret IV
29th to 30th June
Visibility arc 8.8° |{{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|en}} |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |1537 BC |29th Peret I
23rd to 24th January |29th Peret IV
23rd to 24th April |{{ill|9th Schemu III|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=9th Schemu III|lt_de=9. Schemu III}}
2nd to 3rd July |{{ill|Ebers Calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |1465 BC |17th Peret II
24th to 25th January |17th Schemu I
24th to 25th April |27th Schemu III
3rd to 4th July |{{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Astronomical data 28. Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{Ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' 24th to 25th April |27th Schemu III
3rd to 4th July |{{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Astronomical data 28. Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|akronychisch|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic }} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{Ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.''
