24. bis 25. April |27. Schemu III
3. bis 4. Juli |[[Ramessidische Sternuhren]] |- |} == Astronomische Daten 28. Jahrhundert v. Chr. (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]]: [[Akronychisch]]er Untergang am 11. April * [[Sirius]]: Akronychischer Untergang am 12. April, heliakischer Aufgang am 24. Juni (5. [[Heriu-renpet]]) * [[Adhara]]: Akronychischer Untergang am 10. April * Sternbild Sopdet: Akronychischer Untergang am 10. April, heliakischer Aufgang am 24. Juni (5. Heriu-renpet) == Siehe auch == * [[Imi-chet-Sopdet]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. * [[Otto Neugebauer]], [[Richard-Anthony Parker]]: ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Dekansternbild (Altägypten)]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|qq=draft}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|qq=draft}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|qq=draft}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en|qq=draft}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en|qq=draft}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|de|Duat|qq=draft}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|lt=Memphis|qq=draft}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|Season of the Harvest|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II|qq=draft}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet|Heriu-renpet=Heriu-renpet|qq=draft}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine|lt=Elephantine|qq=draft}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
({{ill|Season of the Emergence|de|Peret (Altes Ägypten)|1=Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret|2=de|3=Peret (Altes Ägypten)|4=ja|5=ペレト|6=zh|7=播種季|8=ru|9=Перет|10=uk|11=Перет|qq=draft|13=Season of the Emergence|14=es|15=Estación de la emergencia|16=fr|17=Peret|18=pl|19=Peret|20=ro|21=Peret|lt=9th Peret II|lt_de=9. Peret II|qq=draft}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination|qq=draft}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en|qq=draft}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I
19th to 20th January |11th Achet IV
19th to 20th April |21st Peret II
28th to 29th June |{{ill|Star clock#Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|Star clock=Star clock|lt=Diagonal star clock|qq=draft}} |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |1869 BC |6th Achet III
20th to 21st January

20th to 21st April
Visibility arc 8.6° |16th Peret IV

23rd to 24th January |29th Peret IV
23rd to 24th April |{{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=9th Schemu III|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=9th Schemu III|lt_de=9. Schemu III|qq=draft}}
2nd to 3rd July |{{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft}} |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |1465 BC |17th Peret II

3rd to 4th July |{{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft}} |- |} == Astronomical data 28. Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft}} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft}} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft}}

{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|qq=draft}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|qq=draft}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|qq=draft}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en|qq=draft}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en|qq=draft}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|de|Duat|qq=draft}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|lt=Memphis|qq=draft}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|Season of the Harvest|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II|qq=draft}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet|Heriu-renpet=Heriu-renpet|qq=draft}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine|lt=Elephantine|qq=draft}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
({{ill|Season of the Emergence|de|Peret (Altes Ägypten)|1=Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret|2=de|3=Peret (Altes Ägypten)|4=ja|5=ペレト|6=zh|7=播種季|8=ru|9=Перет|10=uk|11=Перет|qq=draft|13=Season of the Emergence|14=es|15=Estación de la emergencia|16=fr|17=Peret|18=pl|19=Peret|20=ro|21=Peret|lt=9th Peret II|lt_de=9. Peret II|qq=draft}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination|qq=draft}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en|qq=draft}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I
19th to 20th January |11th Achet IV
19th to 20th April |21st Peret II
28th to 29th June |{{ill|Star clock#Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|Star clock=Star clock|lt=Diagonal star clock|qq=draft}} |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |1869 BC |6th Achet III
20th to 21st January

20th to 21st April
Visibility arc 8.6° |16th Peret IV
29th to 30th June
Visibility arc 8.8° |{{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|en|qq=draft}} |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |1537 BC |29th Peret I

This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft}}

({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility

{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}}{{complex}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|qq=draft}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|qq=draft}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|qq=draft}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en|qq=draft}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft}}

Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en|qq=draft}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility

Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I

Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en|qq=draft}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en|qq=draft}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility

April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. ''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|qq=draft}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|qq=draft}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|qq=draft}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en|qq=draft}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft}}

Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}

''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}

Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . == References == {{translated page|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|version=217301816|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] }} [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] [[sn:Sopdet (constellation)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[en:draft:Sopdet (constellation)]] [[sn:opdet (constellation)]] [[de:Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:user:immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] [[en:User:Sopdet (constellation)]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|lt=heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|en|lt_original=Heliakisch|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar|2=ja|3=グレゴリオ暦|4=fr|5=Calendrier grégorien|6=de|7=Gregorianischer Kalender|8=tl|9=Kalendaryong Gregoryano|10=ru|11=Григорианский календарь|de|Gregorianischer Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First night hour|qq=}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth night hour|qq=}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|de|Duat|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duat|qq=}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|lt=Memphis|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Memphis|qq=}}''' |'''13th April'''

({{ill|Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet|Heriu-renpet=Heriu-renpet|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heriu-renpet|qq=}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine|lt=Elephantine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Elephantine|qq=}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
({{ill|Season of the Emergence|de|Peret (Altes Ägypten)|1=Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret|2=de|3=Peret (Altes Ägypten)|4=ja|5=ペレト|6=zh|7=播種季|8=ru|9=Перет|10=uk|11=Перет|qq=draft|13=Season of the Emergence|14=es|15=Estación de la emergencia|16=fr|17=Peret|18=pl|19=Peret|20=ro|21=Peret|lt=9th Peret II|lt_de=9. Peret II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|lt=Acronychal culmination|lt_de=Akronychische Kulmination|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal culmination|qq=}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|lt=Heliacal rising|lt_de=Heliakischer Aufgang|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imi-chet-Sopdet|qq=}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . == References == {{translated page|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|version=217301816|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] }} [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:Sopdet (constellation)]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sopdet (constellation)]][[Category:qq]] [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] [[sn:Sopdet (constellation)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[en:draft:Sopdet (constellation)]] [[sn:opdet (constellation)]] [[de:Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:user:immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] [[en:User:Sopdet (constellation)]]

Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|heliacal rising]] was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ([[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]])''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|heliacal rising]] was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ([[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of [[Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[Proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[Elephantine]] or [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[Pyramid Texts]] of the [[Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ([[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]])''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First night hour|qq=}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|[[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]]
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth night hour|qq=}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
([[:en:Duat|Duat]]) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''[[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]]''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|Season of the Harvest|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet|Heriu-renpet=Heriu-renpet|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heriu-renpet|qq=}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |[[:en:Elephantine|Elephantine]] |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
([[:en:Season of the Emergence|9th Peret II]]) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|[[:en:Culmination|Acronychal culmination]] ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|[[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]]The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I
19th to 20th January |11th Achet IV
19th to 20th April |21st Peret II
28th to 29th June |{{ill|Star clock#Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|Star clock=Star clock|lt=Diagonal star clock|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Star clock#Diagonal star clock|qq=}} |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |1869 BC |6th Achet III
20th to 21st January
Altitude 35.5°Actual culmination 40.7° about 2.25 hours after sunset. |6th Peret II
20th to 21st April
Visibility arc 8.6° |16th Peret IV
29th to 30th June
Visibility arc 8.8° |[[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |1537 BC |29th Peret I
23rd to 24th January |29th Peret IV
23rd to 24th April |{{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=9th Schemu III|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=9th Schemu III|lt_de=9. Schemu III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}
2nd to 3rd July |{{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebers calendar|qq=}} |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |1465 BC |17th Peret II
24th to 25th January |17th Schemu I

Century BC (Memphis) == * [[Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} downfall on April 11th * [[Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imi-chet-Sopdet|qq=}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.''
