''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[:en:Canis Major|Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[:en:Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[:en:Duat|Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[:en:Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[:en:Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|heliacal rising]] was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ([[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[:en:Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[:en:Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[:en:Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[:en:proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[:en:Elephantine|Elephantine]] or [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[:en:Pyramid Texts|Pyramid Texts]] of the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[:en:Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ([[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]])''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}

Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . == References == {{translated page|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|version=217301816|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| }} [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] {{wikidata link|Q1418302}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Qq]]

[[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation [[:en:Canis Major|Canis Major]] and symbolized the deity [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]] onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich [[:en:Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der [[:en:Duat|Duat]] zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely [[:en:Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of [[:en:Nut (goddess)|Nut]]. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|heliacal rising]] was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ([[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|12th Dynasty]]) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]] as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days [[:en:Embalming|embalming time]] ” occurred only within the era from the [[:en:Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|1st.]] [[:en:Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|Dynasty]] until the beginning of the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|3rd]] [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Dynasty]] in the [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] region and the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]] . Due to its [[:en:proper motion|own motion]], Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In [[:en:Elephantine|Elephantine]] or [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The [[:en:Pyramid Texts|Pyramid Texts]] of the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of [[:en:Seti I|Sethos I,]] in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ([[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]])''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First night hour|qq=|WD=Q1361948}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|[[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]]
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth night hour|qq=|WD=Q245250}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
([[:en:Duat|Duat]]) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''[[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]]''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|Season of the Harvest|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|WD=Q1248622}}) |'''23rd June'''

Century BC (Memphis) == * [[:en:Delta Canis Majoris|Wezen]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 11th * [[:en:Sirius|Sirius]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * [[:en:Epsilon Canis Majoris|Adhara]] : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imi-chet-Sopdet|qq=}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation {{ill|Canis Major|en|Canis Major|WD=Q10538|lt=Canis Major}} and symbolized the deity {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}}. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom}} onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}} zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}}. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|WD=Q165496|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=heliacal rising}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ({{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=12th Dynasty}}) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}} as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days {{ill|Embalming|en|Embalming|WD=Q332997|lt=embalming time}} ” occurred only within the era from the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=1st.}} {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=Dynasty}} until the beginning of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=3rd}} {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Dynasty}} in the {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis}} region and the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|WD=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta}} . Due to its {{ill|proper motion|en|proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=own motion}}, Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine}} or {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Thebes}}, however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|WD=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts}} of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}} reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|WD=Q157918|lt=Sethos I,}} in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}

[[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation {{ill|Canis Major|en|Canis Major|WD=Q10538|lt=Canis Major}} and symbolized the deity {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}}. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom}} onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}} zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}}. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|WD=Q165496|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=heliacal rising}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ({{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=12th Dynasty}}) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}} as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days {{ill|Embalming|en|Embalming|WD=Q332997|lt=embalming time}} ” occurred only within the era from the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=1st.}} {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=Dynasty}} until the beginning of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=3rd}} {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Dynasty}} in the {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis}} region and the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|WD=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta}} . Due to its {{ill|proper motion|en|proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=own motion}}, Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine}} or {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Thebes}}, however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|WD=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts}} of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}} reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|WD=Q157918|lt=Sethos I,}} in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First night hour|qq=|WD=Q1361948}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth night hour|qq=|WD=Q245250}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|Season of the Harvest|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|WD=Q1248622}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet|Heriu-renpet=Heriu-renpet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heriu-renpet|qq=|WD=Q1610070}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
({{ill|Season of the Emergence|en|Season of the Emergence|WD=Q2037143|lt=9th Peret II}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Culmination|en|Culmination|WD=Q611114|lt=Acronychal culmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I

Century BC (Memphis) == * {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 11th * {{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|WD=Q3409|lt=Sirius}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|Epsilon Canis Majoris|WD=Q13414|lt=Adhara}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation {{ill|Canis Major|en|Canis Major|WD=Q10538|lt=Canis Major}} and symbolized the deity {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}}. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom}} onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}} zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}}. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|WD=Q165496|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=heliacal rising}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ({{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=12th Dynasty}}) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}} as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days {{ill|Embalming|en|Embalming|WD=Q332997|lt=embalming time}} ” occurred only within the era from the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=1st.}} {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=Dynasty}} until the beginning of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=3rd}} {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Dynasty}} in the {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis}} region and the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|WD=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta}} . Due to its {{ill|proper motion|en|proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=own motion}}, Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine}} or {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Thebes}}, however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|WD=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts}} of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}} reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|WD=Q157918|lt=Sethos I,}} in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First night hour|qq=|WD=Q1361948}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising}}

Century BC (Memphis) == * {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 11th * {{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|WD=Q3409|lt=Sirius}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|Epsilon Canis Majoris|WD=Q13414|lt=Adhara}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imi-chet-Sopdet|qq=}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . == References == {{translated page|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|version=217301816|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| }} [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] {{wikidata link|Q1418302}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

[[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation {{ill|Canis Major|en|Canis Major|WD=Q10538|lt=Canis Major}} and symbolized the deity {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}}. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom}} onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}} zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}}. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|WD=Q165496|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=heliacal rising}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ({{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=12th Dynasty}}) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}} as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days {{ill|Embalming|en|Embalming|WD=Q332997|lt=embalming time}} ” occurred only within the era from the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=1st.}} {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=Dynasty}} until the beginning of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=3rd}} {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Dynasty}} in the {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis}} region and the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|WD=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta}} . Due to its {{ill|proper motion|en|proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=own motion}}, Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine}} or {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Thebes}}, however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|WD=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts}} of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}} reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|WD=Q157918|lt=Sethos I,}} in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First night hour|qq=|WD=Q1361948}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth night hour|qq=|WD=Q245250}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility

Century BC (Memphis) == * {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 11th * {{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|WD=Q3409|lt=Sirius}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|Epsilon Canis Majoris|WD=Q13414|lt=Adhara}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imi-chet-Sopdet|qq=}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . == References == {{translated page|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|version=217301816|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| }} [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] {{wikidata link|Q1418302}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages]]

[[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation {{ill|Canis Major|en|Canis Major|WD=Q10538|lt=Canis Major}} and symbolized the deity {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}}. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom}} onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}} zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}}. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|WD=Q165496|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=heliacal rising}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ({{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=12th Dynasty}}) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}} as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days {{ill|Embalming|en|Embalming|WD=Q332997|lt=embalming time}} ” occurred only within the era from the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=1st.}} {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=Dynasty}} until the beginning of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=3rd}} {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Dynasty}} in the {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis}} region and the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|WD=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta}} . Due to its {{ill|proper motion|en|proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=own motion}}, Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine}} or {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Thebes}}, however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|WD=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts}} of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}} reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|WD=Q157918|lt=Sethos I,}} in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First night hour|qq=|WD=Q1361948}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth night hour|qq=|WD=Q245250}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility
({{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}}) |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |'''2769 BC''' |'''{{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis}}''' |'''13th April'''
({{ill|Season of the Harvest|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=25th Schemu II|lt_de=25. Schemu II|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|WD=Q1248622}}) |'''23rd June'''
({{ill|Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet|Heriu-renpet=Heriu-renpet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heriu-renpet|qq=|WD=Q1610070}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June
(29th Schemu IV) |59 days |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |2137 BC |Memphis |20th April
(1st Achet IV) |28th June
({{ill|Season of the Emergence|en|Season of the Emergence|WD=Q2037143|lt=9th Peret II}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Culmination|en|Culmination|WD=Q611114|lt=Acronychal culmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Notes |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2089 BC |11th Achet I
19th to 20th January |11th Achet IV
19th to 20th April |21st Peret II
28th to 29th June |{{ill|Star clock#Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|Star clock=Star clock|lt=Diagonal star clock|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Star clock#Diagonal star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |1869 BC |6th Achet III
20th to 21st January
Altitude 35.5°Actual culmination 40.7° about 2.25 hours after sunset. |6th Peret II
20th to 21st April
Visibility arc 8.6° |16th Peret IV
29th to 30th June
Visibility arc 8.8° |{{ill|Senusret III|en|Senusret III|WD=Q19248|lt=Senusret III}} |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |1537 BC |29th Peret I
23rd to 24th January |29th Peret IV
23rd to 24th April |{{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=9th Schemu III|de|Schemu#Datierungen|lt=9th Schemu III|lt_de=9. Schemu III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|WD=Q1248622}}
2nd to 3rd July |{{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebers calendar|qq=|WD=Q1279717}} |- style="background:#FFFAF0" |1465 BC |17th Peret II
24th to 25th January |17th Schemu I
24th to 25th April |27th Schemu III
3rd to 4th July |{{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} |- |} == Astronomical data 28. Century BC (Memphis) == * {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 11th * {{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|WD=Q3409|lt=Sirius}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting on 12. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|Epsilon Canis Majoris|WD=Q13414|lt=Adhara}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet) == See also == * {{ill|Imi-chet-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imi-chet-Sopdet|qq=}} == literature == * Christian Leitz : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 . * Otto Neugebauer, Richard-Anthony Parker : ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III – Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * Alexandra von Lieven : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . == References == {{translated page|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|version=217301816|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| }} [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)]] {{wikidata link|Q1418302}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:qq]]

[[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru

''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation {{ill|Canis Major|en|Canis Major|WD=Q10538|lt=Canis Major}} and symbolized the deity {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}}. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom}} onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}} zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen}} and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}}. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|WD=Q165496|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=heliacal rising}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ({{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=12th Dynasty}}) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}} as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days {{ill|Embalming|en|Embalming|WD=Q332997|lt=embalming time}} ” occurred only within the era from the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=1st.}} {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=Dynasty}} until the beginning of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=3rd}} {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Dynasty}} in the {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis}} region and the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|WD=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta}} . Due to its {{ill|proper motion|en|proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=own motion}}, Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine}} or {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Thebes}}, however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|WD=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts}} of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}} reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|WD=Q157918|lt=Sethos I,}} in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1.
