revision.text:
[[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sopdet (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Book of the Day|name=wn:N35:N35-W24:X1-N3:N5-Z1-Z2:N35-h-r:Z7*N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=wenut-net-heru
''wnwt-nt-hrw''
''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation|1=Constellation}} and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation {{ill|Canis Major|en|Canis Major|WD=Q10538|lt=Canis Major|1=Canis Major}} and symbolized the deity {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet|1=Sopdet}}. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom|1=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}} onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen|1=Delta Canis Majoris}} und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat|1=Duat}} zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen|1=Delta Canis Majoris}} and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat|1=Duat}}. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|WD=Q165496|lt=Nut|1=Nut (goddess)}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=heliacal rising|1=Heliacal rising}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ({{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=12th Dynasty|1=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}}) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet|1=Sopdet}} as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days {{ill|Embalming|en|Embalming|WD=Q332997|lt=embalming time|1=Embalming}} ” occurred only within the era from the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=1st.|1=Early Dynastic Period of Egypt}} {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=Dynasty|1=Early Dynastic Period of Egypt}} until the beginning of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=3rd|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Dynasty|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} in the {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis|1=Memphis, Egypt}} region and the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|WD=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta|1=Nile Delta}} . Due to its {{ill|proper motion|en|proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=own motion|1=Proper motion}}, Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine|1=Elephantine}} or {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Thebes|1=Thebes, Egypt}}, however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|WD=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts|1=Pyramid Texts}} of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|WD=Q157918|lt=Sethos I,|1=Seti I}} in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First night hour|qq=|WD=Q1361948|1=First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising|1=Heliacal rising}}
({{ill|Twelfth night hour|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=Twelfth night hour|lt_de=12. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth night hour|qq=|WD=Q245250|1=Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Duration of Invisibility

({{ill|Heriu-renpet|de|Heriu-renpet|lt=5th Heriu-renpet|lt_de=5. Heriu-renpet|Heriu-renpet=Heriu-renpet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heriu-renpet|qq=|WD=Q1610070|1=Heriu-renpet}}) |'''70 days''' |- style="background:#F5F5F5" |2769 BC |{{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine|1=Elephantine}} |18th April
(30th Schemu II) |17th June

''Book of the Day''|TITEL=Sopdet|NAME3=R8-M44-t:N14 N33:N33*N33 Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, S. 104.|NAME3-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet im [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reich]]|NAME3-ERKLÄRUNG=Sopdet
''Spd.t''
''Die gefährliche Göttin''Alexandra von Lieven: '' Wein, Weib und Gesang — Rituale für die Gefährliche Göttin.'' In: Carola Metzner-Nebelsick: ''Rituale in der Vorgeschichte, Antike und Gegenwart – Studien zur Vorderasiatischen, Prähistorischen und Klassischen Archäologie, Ägyptologie, Alten Geschichte, Theologie und Religionswissenschaft; Interdisziplinäre Tagung vom 1.-2. Februar 2002 an der Freien Universität Berlin.'' Leidorf, Rahden 2003, ISBN 3-89646-434-5, S. 47.|NAME4-ERWEITERT=Als Sternbild Sopdet in [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griech.-röm. Zeit]]|NAME4=R8-M44:X1-N14:H8 Heinrich Brugsch: ''Thesaurus inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum – Altägyptische Inschriften – (Reprint 1883).'' Akademie-Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1968, S. 86.}} Sopdet was the ancient Egyptian name for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation|1=Constellation}} and {{ill|Decan|lt=Decan|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|en|lt_original=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=|WD=Q804527}}. The constellation comprised parts of the astronomical constellation {{ill|Canis Major|en|Canis Major|WD=Q10538|lt=Canis Major|1=Canis Major}} and symbolized the deity {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet|1=Sopdet}}. What is striking is the different spelling in hieroglyphs from the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom|1=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}} onwards, as the constellation Sopdet was depicted both with the typical ''t-ending'' and with ''Spd'' . == background == {{HieroglyphenImText|Das Dekansternbild Sopdet besaß gemäß der Beschreibung des [[Nutbuch]]es drei Sterne, [[Seba-en-Sopdet]] („''Stern der Sopdet''“) sowie wahrscheinlich {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen|1=Delta Canis Majoris}} und [[Adhara]]. Das dargestellte Dreieck M44 war in dieser Abbildung am Himmel unmittelbar nach dem Aufgang aus der {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat|1=Duat}} zu sehen. Bei dem [[akronychisch]]en Untergang lag das Dreieck dagegen „auf der Seite“.}}The constellation of Sopdet, according to the description in the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, consisted of three stars, {{ill|Seba-en-Sopdet|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sopdet|qq=}} ("Star of Sopdet"), and likely {{ill|Delta Canis Majoris|en|Delta Canis Majoris|WD=Q13411|lt=Wezen|1=Delta Canis Majoris}} and {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Canis Majoris|qq=}}. The depicted triangleM44was visible in the sky immediately after rising from the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat|1=Duat}}. During the {{ill|acronychic|lt=acronychical|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} setting, however, the triangle lay "on its side." The reasons for the different spelling regarding the ''t-ending'' remain unclear. A possible reason is a ''male-female meaning'' of the Sopdet as a {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|lt=decan star|de|10=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527|Q5248738}} . Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. Following the astronomical conditions, the hieroglyphic writing was different: In the Middle Kingdom, the triangular hieroglyph M44 was still depicted upright, while in Greco-Roman times, due to the changed constellation, the sign M44 was tilted to the left on its tip. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, Sopdet represented the 35th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|WD=Q165496|lt=Nut|1=Nut (goddess)}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=heliacal rising|1=Heliacal rising}} was scheduled for the 16th of {{ill|Season of the Emergence|lt=Peret IV|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Emergence|qq=}} and was based on a decree issued under {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ({{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=12th Dynasty|1=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}}) in his seventh year of reign. This scheduling is a part of ancient Egyptian astronomy where decans were used to mark time during the night, each decan rising consecutively on the horizon throughout the year. [[File:Elfenbeintäfelchen_des_Djer2.PNG|thumb| Ivory tablets of {{ill|Djer|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Djer|qq=}} (earliest evidence of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet|1=Sopdet}} as an annual reference).]] == Invisibility periods of Sirius == The “70 Days {{ill|Embalming|en|Embalming|WD=Q332997|lt=embalming time|1=Embalming}} ” occurred only within the era from the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=1st.|1=Early Dynastic Period of Egypt}} {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|WD=Q187979|lt=Dynasty|1=Early Dynastic Period of Egypt}} until the beginning of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=3rd|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Dynasty|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} in the {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis|1=Memphis, Egypt}} region and the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|WD=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta|1=Nile Delta}} . Due to its {{ill|proper motion|en|proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=own motion|1=Proper motion}}, Sirius was at most 10 hours observable. Aufgangsazimut (Heliakischer Aufgang): Sonne NO 55°, Sirius SO 115° (Differenz etwa 60°); Untergangsazimut (Akronychischer Untergang): Sonne NW 286°, Sirius SW 245° (Differenz etwa 41°). In {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|WD=Q284009|lt=Elephantine|1=Elephantine}} or {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Thebes|1=Thebes, Egypt}}, however, Sirius never stayed 70 days invisible. The {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|WD=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts|1=Pyramid Texts}} of the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} reveal that the Egyptians knew of Sirius's 70-day period of invisibility. In the decan list of {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|WD=Q157918|lt=Sethos I,|1=Seti I}} in connection with the heliacal rising at the time of {{ill|Senusret III|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}, the statement of “70 Days of Invisibility of the Decans”, although explicit reference was made to the dean Sopdet and not to the star Sirius. {|class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight|qq=draft}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Periods of Invisibility of Sirius in Various Regions of Ancient Egypt ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}})''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Observation Location ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}}
({{ill|First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=First night hour|lt_de=1. Nachtstunde|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First night hour|qq=|WD=Q1361948|1=First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}}) ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising|1=Heliacal rising}}

({{ill|Season of the Emergence|en|Season of the Emergence|WD=Q2037143|lt=9th Peret II|1=Season of the Emergence}}) |68 days |- |} == Dating of Sopdet (System of the Nut Book) == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Egyptology TblHighlight}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|Year ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Culmination|en|Culmination|WD=Q611114|lt=Acronychal culmination|1=Culmination}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|lt=Acronychal setting|lt_de=Akronychischer Untergang|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} ! style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising|1=Heliacal rising}}The altitude data refer to the end of the respective night hour. April, heliacal rising on 24 June (5th Heriu-renpet ) * {{ill|Epsilon Canis Majoris|en|Epsilon Canis Majoris|WD=Q13414|lt=Adhara|1=Epsilon Canis Majoris}} : {{ill|acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|simple|User:Immanuelle/acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=|WD=Q4059812}} downfall on April 10 * Constellation Sopdet: Acronymic setting on 10.
